sentiment.label: risk_off
sentiment.reason_it: Il sentiment di mercato è risk-off a causa della guerra tra Israele e Iran, con il VIX a 25.77 e il Brent a $100.81. La Fed e le altre banche centrali saranno sotto pressione per gestire l'inflazione e la stabilità finanziaria. BlackRock Investment Institute e Goldman Sachs Insights hanno pubblicato report settimanali che sottolineano la cautela sui mercati.
sentiment.reason_en: Market sentiment is risk-off due to the Israel-Iran war, with VIX at 25.77 and Brent at $100.81. The Fed and other central banks will be under pressure to manage inflation and financial stability. BlackRock Investment Institute and Goldman Sachs Insights have released weekly reports highlighting market caution.

market_impact_summary.it: La guerra tra Israele e Iran ha aumentato l'incertezza sui mercati, con il VIX in aumento e le borse in calo. Il Brent è salito a $100.81, mentre l'oro è sceso a $5001.50. La Fed e le altre banche centrali dovranno affrontare la sfida di gestire l'inflazione e la stabilità finanziaria. Secondo BlackRock, gli investitori dovrebbero essere cauti sui mercati azionari.
market_impact_summary.en: The Israel-Iran war has increased market uncertainty, with VIX rising and stocks falling. Brent has risen to $100.81, while gold has fallen to $5001.50. The Fed and other central banks will have to manage inflation and financial stability. According to BlackRock, investors should be cautious on equity markets.

audio_script_it:
Benvenuti al consueto briefing mattutino dei mercati. 
Oggi, 16 marzo 2026, il sentiment di mercato è decisamente risk-off, con un indice Vix che sale a 25.77, riflettendo l'incertezza e la preoccupazione degli investitori riguardo alla guerra tra Israele e Iran. Questo conflitto ha fatto sentire i suoi effetti sui mercati finanziari a livello globale, con il prezzo del greggio Brent che raggiunge $100.81, dopo aver guadagnato il 2.67% nella scorsa sessione.

In questo contesto, le banche centrali, tra cui la Federal Reserve, la Banca Centrale Europea e la Banca d'Inghilterra, saranno sotto pressione per gestire l'inflazione e mantenere la stabilità finanziaria. La Fed, in particolare, dovrà prendere in considerazione l'impatto di questo conflitto sui prezzi delle materie prime e sull'economia statunitense.

Passando ai dati di mercato, lo Standard and Poor's 500 ha ceduto lo 0.61%, portandosi a quota 6632 punti, mentre il cambio euro dollaro è sceso dello 0.68% a 1.14. L'ETF obbligazionario Treasury a 20 anni è sceso dello 0.49% a 86.54, e il rendimento del decennale statunitense è salito dello 0.28% a 4.28%.

Il prezzo dell'oro è sceso dell'1.01% a $5001.50 za, mentre Bitcoin si attesta intorno ai settantamila dollari, con un aumento dello 0.69%. L'indice Stoxx seicento europeo ha registrato una lieve flessione dello 0.14% a 59.05, mentre l'indice Nikkei giapponese è sceso dello 0.13% a 53,751 punti.

La liquidità globale, misurata dal proxy mensile Global M2, continua ad espandersi, raggiungendo $22.4 trilioni di dollari, con un aumento del 4.3% su base annua.

Sul fronte macroeconomico, gli ultimi dati disponibili, risalenti al 1 febbraio, mostrano un tasso di inflazione CPI negli Stati Uniti del 2.7% su base annua, in lieve calo rispetto al 2.8% precedente. Il tasso di disoccupazione è salito al 4.4%, mentre le Non-Farm Payrolls hanno registrato un calo di 92,000 unità.

Questa settimana, gli investitori saranno particolarmente attenti alle decisioni delle banche centrali riguardo ai tassi di interesse e alle loro indicazioni sulla gestione dell'inflazione e della stabilità finanziaria. La prossima uscita del PCE Core YoY è prevista per il 28 marzo, mentre il GDP QoQ statunitense sarà pubblicato il 29 aprile.

Gli strategist di Goldman Sachs sottolineano la necessità di cautela sui mercati, data l'incertezza legata al conflitto in Medio Oriente e il suo impatto sull'economia globale. Il team di ricerca di BlackRock Investment Institute consiglia agli investitori di diversificare i loro portafogli e di considerare asset rifugio in caso di escalation del conflitto.

In merito alle notizie del giorno, l'ex presidente degli Stati Uniti Donald Trump ha lanciato un avvertimento agli alleati europei riguardo alla sicurezza nel Mediterraneo e alla possibile minaccia rappresentata dall'Iran. La guerra tra Israele e Iran ha fatto sentire i suoi effetti sui mercati finanziari, con un aumento dei prezzi delle materie prime e una maggiore incertezza.

Il primo ministro del Regno Unito, Starmer, ha annunciato un pacchetto di sostegno energetico del valore di £50 milioni per alleviare l'impatto della guerra sui prezzi dell'energia e sull'inflazione.

Sul fronte geopolitico, la situazione in Ucraina e in Sudan continua a essere critica, con un impatto potenziale sulla stabilità globale.

In conclusione, la guerra tra Israele e Iran e le conseguenti preoccupazioni sull'inflazione e la stabilità finanziaria continueranno a dominare i mercati finanziari nella prossima settimana. Gli investitori dovranno essere pronti a reagire alle notizie e alle decisioni delle banche centrali, mantenendo una strategia diversificata e prudente.

Per quanto riguarda i dati specifici da monitorare, gli investitori dovranno tenere d'occhio le decisioni della Fed, della BCE e della Banca d'Ingilterra sui tassi di interesse, nonché i dati macroeconomici come il PCE Core YoY e il GDP QoQ statunitense. La situazione in Medio Oriente e le sue implicazioni sui mercati finanziari globali rimarranno una priorità assoluta.
audio_script_en:
Welcome to your daily morning market briefing.

Market sentiment is risk-off, scoring 7 out of 10, primarily due to the escalating conflict between Israel and Iran. This geopolitical tension has significantly impacted global markets, with the Vix index at 25.77 and Brent crude oil prices surging to $100.81. The Federal Reserve and other central banks are now under increased pressure to manage inflation and maintain financial stability. Recent reports from the BlackRock Investment Institute and Goldman Sachs Insights have highlighted a cautious market stance.

Let's examine the current market data. The euro-dollar exchange rate stands at 1.14, down 0.68% from the previous day. The Standard and Poor's 500 index is at 6632.19, a 0.61% decrease. The Vix index, often referred to as the fear gauge, is at 25.77, down 5.22% but still elevated. The Treasury bond ETF, which tracks 20-year US Treasury bonds, is at 86.54, down 0.49%. The US 10-year yield is at 4.28%, up 0.28%, indicating a slight increase in interest rates. Gold prices are at $5001.50 per ounce, down 1.01%. Bitcoin, however, has seen a slight increase of 0.69% to $73,290. The Brent crude oil price, as mentioned, is at $100.81, down 2.26%. The Stoxx six hundred index is at 59.05, down 0.14%. The Nikkei index in Japan is at 53,751, down 0.13%, and the Shanghai index in China is at 4084.79, down 0.26%.

In terms of global liquidity, the monthly proxy for Global M2 Liquidity stands at $22.4 trillion, showing an expansion with a year-over-year increase of 4.3% in the US.

Now, let's look at some key macroeconomic data. The last available Consumer Price Index data, released on February 1st, showed a year-over-year increase of 2.7%, slightly down from 2.8% previously. The Core CPI, which excludes volatile food and energy prices, also decreased to 2.7% from 2.9%. Non-Farm Payrolls data showed a significant decrease of 92,000 jobs, compared to an increase of 126,000 previously. The unemployment rate in the US is at 4.4%, up from 4.3%. The Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge, the PCE Core year-over-year, is scheduled for release on March 28th.

The Federal Funds Rate currently stands at 3.64%, unchanged from the previous period. The GDP QoQ data for the US is yet to be released, with the next update scheduled for April 29th.

Geopolitically, the situation in the Middle East continues to escalate. Former US President Trump has warned European allies about NATO, relating to a plea regarding the Hormuz Strait. The ongoing conflict between Israel and Iran has reawakened global inflation fears. Despite this, China's economy has shown signs of rebounding ahead of the Iran crisis.

Central banks are set to make their first rate decisions since the Iran war, with guidance being closely watched. In the UK, there are plans for a £50 million energy support package to ease the fallout from the Iran war. The global implications of this conflict are significant, with many experts weighing in on the potential outcomes.

The Arctic region's security in a changing world has been highlighted as a critical area of concern. The conflict in the Middle East has also raised questions about the potential for escalation or de-escalation in the Gulf region and the prospects for a ceasefire in Lebanon. Other global issues include the ongoing devastating war in Sudan and the challenges of defense tech.

Looking ahead, specific data to watch includes the PCE Core year-over-year on March 28th and the GDP QoQ for the US on April 29th. These releases will provide critical insights into the health of the US economy and the potential trajectory of monetary policy.

In conclusion, the current risk-off sentiment, driven by geopolitical tensions, continues to influence market dynamics. The path forward will likely be shaped by developments in the Middle East and the responses of major central banks. With that in mind, it's essential to stay informed and monitor key data releases in the coming days.

articles.snippet: The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran is entering its third week, with no end yet in sight. Crisis Group experts offer a 360-degree view of its effects on peace, security and economic stability around the world.

articles.snippet: This week on Hold Your Fire!, Richard speaks with Crisis Group’s former President Robert Malley about the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, whether U.S. President Donald Trump will escalate or seek an off-ramp and what Iran might do. He also talks to Crisis Group’s Lebanon expert David Wood about the latest Israel-Hezbollah war.

articles.title: The Danger of Vibe Patriotism in Defense Tech
articles.snippet: At conferences, in pitch decks, and increasingly in public writing, “service” is increasingly being used to describe the work of startup founders, employees, and venture capital investors in defense technology. The claim, sometimes explicit, more often implied, is that building defense technology is itself a form of service comparable to uniformed military service.It is visible in corporate messaging that invites civilians to “serve” through product work, from Palantir’s “Why We Serve” to Anduril’s description of “national service as a form of innovation,” to public-private innovation narratives that describe entrepreneurship as a form of national service and “answering the call” advocacy The post The Danger of Vibe Patriotism in Defense Tech appeared first on War on the Rocks .

articles.title: Sinopec Slashes Refining Runs as Hormuz Disruption Squeezes Crude Supply
articles.snippet: Sinopec, China’s biggest oil refiner, has reduced its run rates by 10%, Bloomberg reported today, citing unnamed sources, in response to the supply squeeze resulting from the traffic disruption in the Strait of Hormuz. The size of the cut is equal to about half a million barrels daily. There will also be additional output losses from maintenance operations, the sources said. The refining major accounts for about a third of China’s total refined petroleum product output, with an average processing rate of 5.2 million barrels daily, the…

articles.title: Middle East Crisis Could Triple Pakistan’s Oil Import Bill
articles.snippet: Previously, we reported that Pakistan could find itself sucked into the Middle East war thanks to the Strategic Mutual Defence Agreement (SMDA) it signed with Saudi Arabia in September 2025. The core of the agreement states that any aggression against one country is considered an aggression against both, potentially requiring Pakistan to intervene against Iran’s missile strikes on Saudi Arabia. However, military confrontation with its southerly neighbor might turn out to be a less existential threat for Pakistan. Surging oil prices are already…

articles.title: How the Iran War Could Reshape Global Energy Policy
articles.snippet: The U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran and the ongoing conflict in the Middle East have caused the biggest oil disruption in history. The closure of a major transit route between Asia and Europe, as well as restrictions on fossil fuel production in the region, have driven oil and gas prices up, a trend that is set to continue. So, as governments are facing rising consumer energy bills once again, will it be enough to encourage a Covid-era pivot to renewable energy? The U.S. war against Iran has caused the largest oil disruption in history, according to…

articles.title: Oil Prices Remain Volatile as Hormuz Tensions Rise
articles.snippet: Oil markets remained volatile in early Asian trade on Monday as Trump attempts to form a broader coalition of countries to protect shipping through the Strait of Hormuz amid fresh attacks on energy infrastructure across the Middle East. At the time of writing, West Texas Intermediate had dipped 0.53% to $98.19 after an initial surge, while Brent was continuing its climb, rising 0.41% to $103.60 per barrel. The volatility follows a weekend of escalation in the Middle East conflict, beginning on Friday evening when the U.S. bombed military assets…

articles.title: EU Ministers Race to Shield Industry From an Escalating Energy Shock
articles.snippet: The European Union is in a rush to find ways to alleviate the energy cost pain for consumers and businesses, with energy ministers of member states set to meet later today to discuss options. These, according to Reuters, include boosting the supply of carbon permits to reduce their price, state support for industrial energy consumers, and tax cuts. The reportedly discussed options suggest European leaders are making a rare move to prioritize energy affordability over emission reduction. The option with greater availability of carbon permits is…
